97	\section{Mayer-Vietoris LES building blocks: \texorpdfstring{$\toBiprod$}{toBiprod} and \texorpdfstring{$\fromBiprod$}{fromBiprod} (iter-017)}
98	\label{sec:hmodule_prime_mayer_vietoris_first}
99	
100	After Definitions~\ref{def:Scheme_HModule_prime}--\ref{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_cohomologyPresheaf} the cohomology-of-an-open carrier $\HModulePrime\,k\,F\,n\,X$, its $H^0$ identification, and its contravariant-in-$X$ functoriality (assembled into the presheaf $\HModulePrime\_\cohomologyPresheaf\,k\,F\,n$) are all in scope. Iter-017 attacks the Mayer-Vietoris long exact sequence on the $\ModuleCat\,k$ side directly, mirroring Mathlib's \texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/SheafCohomology/MayerVietoris.lean} (file authored by J.~Riou) line-by-line for $\Sheaf\,J\,\AddCommGrpCat$-valued sheaves but with $F.\cohomologyPresheaf$ replaced by $\HModulePrime\_\cohomologyPresheaf\,k\,F$. The first two LES building blocks are the sum and difference of two restriction maps; both are one-line term-mode \texttt{biprod.lift} / \texttt{biprod.desc} bodies, probe-confirmed by the iter-017 plan-agent.
101	
102	\subsection{Setup: Mayer-Vietoris squares}
103	
104	A \emph{Mayer-Vietoris square} for a Grothendieck topology $J$ on a category $C$ is, informally, a pullback square in $C$
105	\[
106	  \begin{tikzcd}
107	    X_1 \ar[r, "f_{1,2}"] \ar[d, "f_{1,3}"'] & X_2 \ar[d, "f_{2,4}"] \\
108	    X_3 \ar[r, "f_{3,4}"'] & X_4
109	  \end{tikzcd}
110	\]
111	that is a $J$-cover (in the sense that the pair $\{f_{2,4}, f_{3,4}\} : X_2 \sqcup X_3 \to X_4$ is a covering family for $J$, and the pullback to $X_1$ is the obvious one). Mathlib's $\mathtt{GrothendieckTopology.MayerVietorisSquare}$ (file \texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/MayerVietorisSquare.lean}) bundles this datum together with its sheaf-theoretic exactness properties; we use it as a black box.
112	
113	For us, the canonical example is a 2-affine open cover $X_4 = U_2 \cup U_3$ of an open $X_4 \subseteq C.\mathrm{left}.\mathrm{toTopCat}$ on a curve $C$, with $U_1 = U_2 \cap U_3$. After iter-017 + iter-018 + iter-019 land the LES theorem, this specialisation will be the engine of the Path-2 Serre-finiteness argument (`iter-020+`).
114	
115	\subsection{First LES building block: the sum of two restriction maps}
116	
117	\begin{definition}[Sum of two restriction maps in MV cohomology, $\HModulePrime$ flavor (iter-017)]
118	  \label{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_toBiprod}
119	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule'_toBiprod}
120	  \leanok
121	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule_prime, def:Scheme_HModule_prime_cohomologyPresheaf}
122	  Let $k$ be a field, $C$ a category with $J : \mathrm{GrothendieckTopology}\,C$, and assume $\HasWeakSheafify\,J\,(\mathrm{Type}\,u)$, $\HasSheafify\,J\,(\ModuleCat.\{u\}\,k)$, and $\HasExt(\Sheaf(J, \ModuleCat.\{u\}\,k))$. Let $S : J.\MayerVietorisSquare$, $F : \Sheaf(J, \ModuleCat.\{u\}\,k)$, and $n : \mathbb N$. Define
123	  \[
124	    \HModulePrime\_\toBiprod\,k\,S\,F\,n \;\colon\;
125	    (\HModulePrime\_\cohomologyPresheaf\,k\,F\,n).\mathrm{obj}\,(\mathrm{op}\,S.X_4)
126	    \longrightarrow
127	    (\HModulePrime\_\cohomologyPresheaf\,k\,F\,n).\mathrm{obj}\,(\mathrm{op}\,S.X_2)
128	    \boxplus
129	    (\HModulePrime\_\cohomologyPresheaf\,k\,F\,n).\mathrm{obj}\,(\mathrm{op}\,S.X_3)
130	  \]
131	  by
132	  \[
133	    \HModulePrime\_\toBiprod\,k\,S\,F\,n \;:=\;
134	      \mathrm{biprod.lift}\bigl((\HModulePrime\_\cohomologyPresheaf\,k\,F\,n).\mathrm{map}\,S.f_{2,4}.\mathtt{op},\;\;(\HModulePrime\_\cohomologyPresheaf\,k\,F\,n).\mathrm{map}\,S.f_{3,4}.\mathtt{op}\bigr).
135	  \]
136	\end{definition}
137	
138	\begin{proof}
139	  \leanok
140	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_cohomologyPresheaf}
141	  Direct mirror of Mathlib's $\mathtt{GrothendieckTopology.MayerVietorisSquare.toBiprod}$ (\texttt{MayerVietoris.lean} L43--46) with $F.\cohomologyPresheaf \rightarrow \HModulePrime\_\cohomologyPresheaf\,k\,F$. The biproduct $\boxplus$ is the standard biproduct in $\AddCommGrpCat$ (Mathlib `Mathlib/Algebra/Category/Grp/Biproducts.lean`); it is well-defined because $\AddCommGrpCat$ has finite biproducts.
142	  Plan-agent \texttt{lean\_run\_code} probe (iter-017) confirms the body typechecks end-to-end against current Mathlib + post-iter-016 project state, kernel-only axioms (\lstinline{propext}, \lstinline{Classical.choice}, \lstinline{Quot.sound}). The closure body is the one-line term \lstinline{biprod.lift ((HModule'_cohomologyPresheaf k F n).map S.f₂₄.op) ((HModule'_cohomologyPresheaf k F n).map S.f₃₄.op)}.
143	\end{proof}
144	
145	\subsection{Second LES building block: the difference of two restriction maps}
146	
147	\begin{definition}[Difference of two restriction maps in MV cohomology, $\HModulePrime$ flavor (iter-017)]
148	  \label{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_fromBiprod}
149	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule'_fromBiprod}
150	  \leanok
151	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule_prime, def:Scheme_HModule_prime_cohomologyPresheaf}
152	  In the setting of Definition~\ref{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_toBiprod}, define
153	  \[
154	    \HModulePrime\_\fromBiprod\,k\,S\,F\,n \;\colon\;
155	    (\HModulePrime\_\cohomologyPresheaf\,k\,F\,n).\mathrm{obj}\,(\mathrm{op}\,S.X_2)
156	    \boxplus
157	    (\HModulePrime\_\cohomologyPresheaf\,k\,F\,n).\mathrm{obj}\,(\mathrm{op}\,S.X_3)
158	    \longrightarrow
159	    (\HModulePrime\_\cohomologyPresheaf\,k\,F\,n).\mathrm{obj}\,(\mathrm{op}\,S.X_1)
160	  \]
161	  by
162	  \[
163	    \HModulePrime\_\fromBiprod\,k\,S\,F\,n \;:=\;
164	      \mathrm{biprod.desc}\bigl((\HModulePrime\_\cohomologyPresheaf\,k\,F\,n).\mathrm{map}\,S.f_{1,2}.\mathtt{op},\;\;-(\HModulePrime\_\cohomologyPresheaf\,k\,F\,n).\mathrm{map}\,S.f_{1,3}.\mathtt{op}\bigr).
165	  \]

168	\begin{proof}
169	  \leanok
170	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_cohomologyPresheaf}
171	  Direct mirror of Mathlib's $\mathtt{GrothendieckTopology.MayerVietorisSquare.fromBiprod}$ (\texttt{MayerVietoris.lean} L67--70) with $F.\cohomologyPresheaf \rightarrow \HModulePrime\_\cohomologyPresheaf\,k\,F$. The negation $-(\HModulePrime\_\cohomologyPresheaf\,k\,F\,n).\mathrm{map}\,S.f_{1,3}.\mathtt{op}$ is the additive inverse in the additive structure of $\AddCommGrpCat$-morphisms (the codomain category is $\AddCommGrpCat$ which is preadditive, so morphisms have additive inverses); the sign is the standard convention from the Mayer-Vietoris LES, encoding the alternating-sum structure of the Čech complex.

The closure body is the one-line term \lstinline{biprod.desc ((HModule'_cohomologyPresheaf k F n).map S.f₁₂.op) (-(HModule'_cohomologyPresheaf k F n).map S.f₁₃.op)}.

174	
175	\begin{remark}[Why these are the iter-017 targets]
176	  The Mayer-Vietoris LES theorem (Mathlib \texttt{MayerVietoris.lean} L120--141) constructs a six-term sequence
177	  \[
178	    \cdots \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_4, F) \xrightarrow{\;\toBiprod\;} H^n(S.X_2, F) \boxplus H^n(S.X_3, F) \xrightarrow{\;\fromBiprod\;} H^n(S.X_1, F) \xrightarrow{\;\delta\;} H^{n+1}(S.X_4, F) \longrightarrow \cdots
179	  \]
180	  whose three building blocks are $\toBiprod$, $\fromBiprod$, and the connecting hom $\delta$. The first two are direct one-line term-mode \texttt{biprod.lift} / \texttt{biprod.desc} bodies on the cohomology presheaf's restriction maps; iter-017 lands them. Iter-018 proves the composition-is-zero lemma $\toBiprod \circ \fromBiprod = 0$ (Mathlib L72--75) — the verbatim Mathlib \lstinline{simp only} proof transfers cleanly because every rewrite step is value-category-agnostic (see Section~\ref{sec:hmodule_prime_mayer_vietoris_compose_zero}). The connecting hom $\delta$ requires the extension class machinery \lstinline{Ext.precomp} (Mathlib \texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Homology/DerivedCategory/Ext/ExactSequences.lean}) applied to the Mayer-Vietoris square's induced short exact sequence; iter-019+ will scope it because Mathlib's \lstinline{MayerVietorisSquare.shortComplex_shortExact} (Mathlib \texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/MayerVietorisSquare.lean} L240--268) is built from \lstinline{AddCommGrpCat.free}-yoneda — the $\ModuleCat\,k$ flavour requires a parallel construction with \lstinline{ModuleCat.free k}-yoneda, which is substantive multi-iteration work. Iter-019/020+ then lands the LES sequence itself, the iso to $\Ext.\mathrm{contravariantSequence}$ (Mathlib L120--141), and the exactness theorem.
181	\end{remark}
182	
183	\begin{remark}[Compatibility with Mathlib's $\AddCommGrpCat$-flavored LES]
184	  By construction, the codomain $\AddCommGrpCat$ of $\HModulePrime\_\cohomologyPresheaf$ matches Mathlib's $F.\cohomologyPresheaf$ codomain (forced by $\Abelian.\extFunctor\,n$ always landing in $\AddCommGrpCat$). The biproduct $\boxplus$ in $\AddCommGrpCat$, the morphism additive structure, and the negation are all the same in both worlds. The only difference is the source of the cohomology presheaf: $F : \Sheaf\,J\,\AddCommGrpCat$ in Mathlib vs.\ $F : \Sheaf\,J\,(\ModuleCat.\{u\}\,k)$ in our setup. In particular, every Mathlib-level proof on $\toBiprod$ / $\fromBiprod$ (e.g.\ the composition-is-zero lemma at L72--75) transports verbatim to our setting once we replace `F.cohomologyPresheaf` with `HModule'_cohomologyPresheaf k F`; iter-018 uses this directly (Section~\ref{sec:hmodule_prime_mayer_vietoris_compose_zero}).
185	\end{remark}
186	
187	\section{Mayer-Vietoris LES building block: composition-is-zero (iter-018)}
188	\label{sec:hmodule_prime_mayer_vietoris_compose_zero}
189	
190	After Definitions~\ref{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_toBiprod}--\ref{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_fromBiprod} the two MV-LES building blocks $\toBiprod$ and $\fromBiprod$ are in scope. Iter-018 proves the third LES building block — the composition-is-zero relation $\toBiprod \circ \fromBiprod = 0$, which is one of the two zero-composition lemmas underpinning the eventual exactness theorem (the other being $\delta \circ \toBiprod = 0$, queued iter-019+).
191	
192	\begin{lemma}[Composition $\toBiprod \circ \fromBiprod$ vanishes (iter-018)]
193	  \label{lem:Scheme_HModule_prime_toBiprod_fromBiprod}
194	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule'_toBiprod_fromBiprod}
195	  \leanok
196	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_toBiprod, def:Scheme_HModule_prime_fromBiprod}
197	  In the setting of Definition~\ref{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_toBiprod}, the composition
198	  \[
199	    \HModulePrime\_\toBiprod\,k\,S\,F\,n \;\circ\; \HModulePrime\_\fromBiprod\,k\,S\,F\,n
200	    \;=\; 0
201	  \]
202	  in the morphism set $(\HModulePrime\_\cohomologyPresheaf\,k\,F\,n).\mathrm{obj}\,(\mathrm{op}\,S.X_4) \to (\HModulePrime\_\cohomologyPresheaf\,k\,F\,n).\mathrm{obj}\,(\mathrm{op}\,S.X_1)$.
203	\end{lemma}
204	
205	\begin{proof}
206	  \leanok
207	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_toBiprod, def:Scheme_HModule_prime_fromBiprod}
208	  Direct mirror of Mathlib's $\mathtt{GrothendieckTopology.MayerVietorisSquare.toBiprod\_fromBiprod}$ (\texttt{MayerVietoris.lean} L72--75). The proof reads as the composition of seven verbatim rewrites in a single \lstinline{simp only} block:
209	  \begin{enumerate}
210	    \item \lstinline{[HModule'_toBiprod, HModule'_fromBiprod]}: unfold the two iter-017 LES building blocks to expose the \lstinline{biprod.lift} / \lstinline{biprod.desc} shape.
211	    \item \lstinline{[biprod.lift_desc]}: apply the biproduct universal property
212	      \[ \mathrm{biprod.lift}\,a\,b \;\circ\; \mathrm{biprod.desc}\,c\,d \;=\; (a \circ c) + (b \circ d). \]
213	    \item \lstinline{[Preadditive.comp_neg]}: push the negation in $\fromBiprod$'s second argument through the composition.
214	    \item \lstinline{[← sub_eq_add_neg]}: rewrite the resulting $a + (-b)$ as $a - b$.
215	    \item \lstinline{[sub_eq_zero]}: reduce $a - b = 0$ to $a = b$ in the ambient \lstinline{AddCommGroup}.
216	    \item \lstinline{[← Functor.map_comp, ← op_comp]}: fuse the two consecutive applications of $(\HModulePrime\_\cohomologyPresheaf\,k\,F\,n).\mathrm{map}$ via contravariant functoriality (the $\mathrm{op}$ direction makes the morphism order swap).

240	\section{Mayer-Vietoris LES short-complex infrastructure (iter-019)}
241	\label{sec:hmodule_prime_mayer_vietoris_short_complex}
242	
243	Iter-019 lays the carrier of the eventual $\Sheaf\,J\,(\ModuleCat\,k)$-flavored short exact sequence on which the connecting hom $\delta$ of the Mayer-Vietoris LES is built. The short complex is a direct mirror of Mathlib's $\AddCommGrpCat$-flavored \lstinline{MayerVietorisSquare.shortComplex} (\texttt{MayerVietorisSquare.lean} L234--249), with $\AddCommGrpCat.\free$ replaced by $\ModuleCat.\free\,k$. The infrastructure decomposes into three declarations: a one-line Mathlib gap-fill instance, a pushout-of-free-sheaves analog (lemma), and the short-complex carrier (definition).
244	
245	\subsection{Mathlib gap and the helper instance}
246	
247	Mathlib registers $(\AddCommGrpCat.\free).\IsLeftAdjoint$ in \lstinline{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/Grp/Adjunctions.lean} L84 as a typeclass instance, enabling $\Sheaf.\composeAndSheafify\,J\,\AddCommGrpCat.\free$ to acquire the necessary $\PreservesColimit$ instance. Mathlib's \lstinline{ModuleCat/Adjunctions.lean} provides the adjunction \lstinline{ModuleCat.adj k : ModuleCat.free k ⊣ forget (ModuleCat k)} but does not register the corresponding $\IsLeftAdjoint$ instance (that registration lives only in \lstinline{ModuleCat/Topology/Basic.lean} for topological modules, an unrelated context). This is a real Mathlib gap on the path to a $\ModuleCat\,k$-flavored Mayer-Vietoris LES.

249	The iter-019 helper fills the gap with a one-line instance:

251	\begin{definition}[Helper instance: $\ModuleCat.\free\,k$ is left adjoint (iter-019)]
252	  \label{def:Scheme_ModuleCat_free_isLeftAdjoint}
253	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ModuleCat_free_isLeftAdjoint}
254	  \leanok
255	  \uses{}
256	  Let $k : \mathrm{Type}\,u$ be a field. Then $\ModuleCat.\free\,k$ is a left adjoint:
257	  \[
258	    (\ModuleCat.\free\,k).\IsLeftAdjoint \;:=\; \langle \_, \langle \ModuleCat.\mathtt{adj}\,k \rangle\rangle.
259	  \]
260	\end{definition}
261	
262	\begin{proof}
263	  \leanok
264	  \uses{}
265	  The body is a structural term-mode value packaging Mathlib's existing \lstinline{ModuleCat.adj k : ModuleCat.free k ⊣ forget (ModuleCat k)} into the typeclass-witness form expected by \lstinline{Functor.IsLeftAdjoint}. No proof tactics; just typeclass registration. 268	\subsection{The pushout-of-free-sheaves analog}
269	
270	\begin{lemma}[Pushout of free $\ModuleCat\,k$-sheaves under a Mayer-Vietoris square (iter-019)]
271	  \label{lem:Scheme_HModule_prime_isPushoutModuleCatFreeSheaf}
272	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule'_isPushoutModuleCatFreeSheaf}
273	  \leanok
274	  \uses{def:Scheme_ModuleCat_free_isLeftAdjoint}
275	  Let $k$ be a field, $C$ a category with $J : \mathrm{GrothendieckTopology}\,C$, and assume $\HasWeakSheafify\,J\,(\mathrm{Type}\,u)$ and $\HasSheafify\,J\,(\ModuleCat.\{u\}\,k)$. Let $S : J.\MayerVietorisSquare$. Then the image of $S$ under the composite
276	  \[
277	    \yoneda \;\circ\; \Functor.\whiskeringRight\,(\ModuleCat.\free\,k) \;\circ\; \presheafToSheaf\,J\,\_
278	  \]
279	  is a pushout square in $\Sheaf\,J\,(\ModuleCat\,k)$.
280	\end{lemma}
281	
282	\begin{proof}
283	  \leanok
284	  \uses{def:Scheme_ModuleCat_free_isLeftAdjoint}
285	  Direct mirror of Mathlib's $\mathtt{GrothendieckTopology.MayerVietorisSquare.isPushoutAddCommGrpFreeSheaf}$ (\texttt{MayerVietorisSquare.lean} L154--160) with $\AddCommGrpCat.\free \rightarrow \ModuleCat.\free\,k$. The proof reads as: (1) start with $S.\isPushout$, the type-level pushout; (2) push it through $\Sheaf.\composeAndSheafify\,J\,(\ModuleCat.\free\,k)$ (which preserves pushouts because $\ModuleCat.\free\,k$ is a left adjoint, registered by Definition~\ref{def:Scheme_ModuleCat_free_isLeftAdjoint}); (3) translate the resulting square's shape via the canonical iso $\presheafToSheaf\composeAndSheafify\mathrm{Iso}$ between two ways of writing $\presheafToSheaf\,J\,\_ \circ (\yoneda \circ \mathrm{whiskerRight}\,\free)$. Body:
286	  \begin{lstlisting}
287	  (S.isPushout.map (Sheaf.composeAndSheafify J (ModuleCat.free k))).of_iso
288	    ((Square.mapFunctor.mapIso
289	      (presheafToSheafCompComposeAndSheafifyIso J (ModuleCat.free k))).app
290	        (S.map yoneda))
291	  \end{lstlisting}
292	  Plan-agent \texttt{lean\_run\_code} probe (iter-019) confirms the body typechecks end-to-end against current Mathlib + post-iter-018 project state, kernel-only axioms (\lstinline{propext}, \lstinline{Classical.choice}, \lstinline{Quot.sound}). Every component (\lstinline{Sheaf.composeAndSheafify}, \lstinline{Square.IsPushout.map}, \lstinline{Square.IsPushout.of_iso}, \lstinline{presheafToSheafCompComposeAndSheafifyIso}, \lstinline{Square.mapFunctor.mapIso}) is value-category-agnostic in Mathlib; the proof transfers cleanly.
293	\end{proof}

The localization functor $Q$ maps $\varphi$ to an isomorphism in the derived category if and only if $\varphi$ is a quasi-isomorphism (i.e., it induces isomorphisms on all homology groups)."},{"full_name":"CochainComplex.quasiIsoAt_shift_iff","formal_statement":"theorem CochainComplex.quasiIsoAt_shift_iff {K L : CochainComplex C ℤ} (φ : K ⟶ L) (n i j : ℤ) (h : n + i = j) : QuasiIsoAt (φ⟦n⟧') i ↔ QuasiIsoAt φ j := by
  simp only [quasiIsoAt_iff_isIso_homologyMap]
  exact (NatIso.isIso_map_iff

For integers $n$, $i$, and $j$ such that $n + i = j$, the shifted chain map $\varphi \llbracket n \rrbracket$ is a quasi-isomorphism in degree $i$ if and only if $\varphi$ is a quasi-isomorphism in degree $j$."},{"full_name":"HomologicalComplex.mem_quasiIso_iff","formal_statement":"theorem HomologicalComplex.mem_quasiIso_iff (f : K ⟶ L) : quasiIso C c f ↔ QuasiIso f := by rfl","informal_statement":"A chain map $f \colon K \to L$ between homological complexes in a category $C$ with shape $c$ satisfies the quasi-isomorphism property (i.e., $f \in \text{quasiIso}\, C\, c$) if and only if $f$ is a quasi-isomorphism, meaning that for every degree $i$, the induced map on homology $H_i(f) \colon H_i(K) \to H_i(L)$ is an isomorphism."},{"full_name":"quasiIso_iff_of_arrow_mk_iso","formal_statement":"theorem quasiIso_iff_of_arrow_mk_iso (φ : K ⟶ L) (φ' : K' ⟶ L') (e : Arrow.mk φ ≅ Arrow.mk φ') [∀ i, K.HasHomology i] [∀ i, L.HasHomology i]
  [∀ i, K'.HasHomology i] [∀ i, L'.HasHomology i] : QuasiIso φ ↔ QuasiIso φ' := by
  simp [← quasiIso_iff_comp_left (show K' ⟶ K from e.inv.left) φ,
    ← quasiIso_iff_comp_right φ' (show L' ⟶ L from e.inv.right)]","informal_statement":"Let $\phi \colon K \to L$ and $\phi' \colon K' \to L'$ be chain maps between homological complexes, and suppose there exists an isomorphism $e$ in the arrow category between $\mathrm{Arrow.mk}(\phi)$ and $\mathrm{Arrow.mk}(\phi')$. If all complexes involved have homology defined in every degree, then $\phi$ is a quasi-isomorphism if and only if $\phi'$ is a quasi-isomorphism."},{"full_name":"quasiIso_of_arrow_mk_iso","formal_statement":"theorem quasiIso_of_arrow_mk_iso (φ : K ⟶ L) (φ' : K' ⟶ L') (e : Arrow.mk φ ≅ Arrow.mk φ') [∀ i, K.HasHomology i] [∀ i, L.HasHomology i]
  [∀ i, K'.HasHomology i] [∀ i, L'.HasHomology i] [hφ : QuasiIso φ] : QuasiIso φ' := by
  simpa only [← quasiIso_iff_of_arrow_mk_iso φ φ' e]","informal_statement":"Let $\phi \colon K \to L$ and $\phi' \colon K' \to L'$ be chain maps between homological complexes, and suppose there exists an isomorphism $e$ in the arrow category between $\mathrm{Arrow.mk}(\phi)$ and $\mathrm{Arrow.mk}(\phi')$. If all complexes involved have homology defined in every degree and $\phi$ is a quasi-isomorphism (i.e., it induces isomorphisms on homology in all degrees), then $\phi'$ is also a quasi-isomorphism."}]}

124	in the opposite category. -/
125	def homotopyUnop (h : Homotopy ((opEquivalence C).functor.map f.op)
126	    ((opEquivalence C).functor.map g.op)) :
127	    Homotopy f g where
128	  hom p q := (K.XIsoOfEq (by simp)).hom ≫ (h.hom (-q) (-p)).unop ≫ (L.XIsoOfEq (by simp)).hom
129	  zero p q hpq := by
130	    rw [h.zero, unop_zero, zero_comp, comp_zero]
131	    dsimp at hpq ⊢
132	    lia
133	  comm n := Quiver.Hom.op_inj (by
134	    have H (p q p' q' : ℤ) (hp : p = p') (hq : q = q') :
135	      h.hom p q = (L.XIsoOfEq (by simpa using hp.symm)).hom.op ≫ h.hom p' q' ≫
136	        (K.XIsoOfEq (by simpa)).hom.op := by
137	      subst hp hq
138	      simp
139	    obtain ⟨n, rfl⟩ : ∃ (m : ℤ), n = -m := ⟨-n , by simp⟩
140	    have := h.comm n
141	    dsimp at this
142	    rw [op_add, op_add, this, add_left_inj, add_comm]
143	    congr 1
144	    · refine (prevD_eq _ (j' := n - 1) (by dsimp; lia)).trans ?_
145	      rw [dNext_eq _ (i' := - (n - 1)) (by dsimp; lia)]
146	      dsimp
147	      simp [H (- -n) (- -(n - 1)) n (n - 1) (by lia) (by lia), ← op_comp_assoc]
148	    · refine (dNext_eq _ (i' := n + 1) (by dsimp)).trans ?_
149	      rw [prevD_eq _ (j' := - (n + 1)) (by simp)]
150	      dsimp
151	      simp [H (- -(n + 1)) (- -n) (n + 1) n (by simp) (by simp), ← op_comp_assoc, ← op_comp])
152	
153	lemma homotopyUnop_hom_eq
154	    (h : Homotopy ((opEquivalence C).functor.map f.op)
155	      ((opEquivalence C).functor.map g.op))
156	    (p q p' q' : ℤ) (hp : p + p' = 0 := by lia) (hq : q + q' = 0 := by lia) :
157	    (homotopyUnop h).hom p q =
158	      (K.XIsoOfEq (by dsimp; lia)).hom ≫ (h.hom q' p').unop ≫
159	        (L.XIsoOfEq (by dsimp; lia)).hom := by
160	  obtain rfl : p' = -p := by lia
161	  obtain rfl : q' = -q := by lia
162	  simp [homotopyUnop]
163	
164	end

166	/-- Two morphisms of cochain complexes indexed by `ℤ` are homotopic iff
167	they are homotopic after the application of the functor
168	`(opEquivalence C).functor : (CochainComplex C ℤ)ᵒᵖ ⥤ CochainComplex Cᵒᵖ ℤ`. -/
169	def homotopyOpEquiv {K L : CochainComplex C ℤ} {f g : K ⟶ L} :
170	    Homotopy f g ≃ Homotopy ((opEquivalence C).functor.map f.op)
171	      ((opEquivalence C).functor.map g.op) where
172	  toFun h := homotopyOp h
173	  invFun h := homotopyUnop h
174	  left_inv h := by
175	    ext p q
176	    simp [homotopyUnop_hom_eq _ p q (-p) (-q),
177	      homotopyOp_hom_eq _ (-q) (-p) q p]
178	  right_inv h := by
179	    ext p q
180	    simp [homotopyOp_hom_eq _ p q (-p) (-q),
181	      homotopyUnop_hom_eq _ (-q) (-p) q p]

184	    (m : ℤ) (hm : n + m = 0 := by lia) :
185	    ((opEquivalence C).functor.obj (op K)).ExactAt m := by
186	  obtain rfl : n = -m := by lia
187	  rw [HomologicalComplex.exactAt_iff' _ (m - 1) m (m + 1) (by simp) (by simp),

189	  rwa [HomologicalComplex.exactAt_iff' _ (-(m + 1)) (-m) (-(m - 1)) (by grind [prev])

191

194	  fun n ↦ exactAt_op (hK (-n)) n

But wait, `CochainComplex.exactAt_op` goes from `CochainComplex C ℤ` to `CochainComplex Cᵒᵖ ℤ`. Our Čech complex is indexed by `ℕ`, not `ℤ`. Let me check if there's a similar result for `ℕ`-indexed complexes.

Actually, let me look at the `cechComplexFunctor` definition more carefully. It's a cochain complex indexed by `ℕ`, not `ℤ`. The `opEquivalence` is for `ℤ`-indexed complexes.

Hmm, this is a problem. The extra-degeneracy machinery works on `SimplicialObject.Augmented` which gives `AlternatingFaceMapComplex` (a chain complex indexed by `ℕ`, or equivalently `ℤ` with zeros in negative degrees). We need to convert this to our `ℕ`-indexed cochain complex.

6	module
7	
8	public import Mathlib.AlgebraicTopology.AlternatingFaceMapComplex
9	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Limits.FormalCoproducts.Cech
10	
11	/-!
12	# Cech cohomology
13	
14	Given a family of objects `U : ι → C` in a category `C` that has finite products,
15	we define a Cech complex functor
16	`cechComplexFunctor : (Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A) ⥤ CochainComplex A ℕ` which sends a presheaf
17	`P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A` in a preadditive category (where products exist) to the cochain
18	complex which in degree `n` consists of the product, indexed by `i : Fin (n + 1) → ι`,
19	of the value of `P` on the product of the objects `U (i a)` for `a : Fin (n + 1)`.
20	
21	-/
22	
23	@[expose] public section
24	
25	universe w t v v' u u'
26	
27	namespace CategoryTheory
28	
29	open Limits
30	
31	variable {C : Type u} [Category.{v} C] {A : Type u'} [Category.{v'} A] [HasProducts.{w} A]
32	
33	namespace Limits.FormalCoproduct
34	
35	open Opposite
36	
37	variable (E : SimplicialObject (FormalCoproduct.{w} C))
38	
39	/-- Given a simplicial object `E` in the category `FormalCoproduct C`, this is the
40	functor `(Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A) ⥤ CosimplicialObject A` which sends `P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A` to the
41	cosimplicial object which sends `⦋n⦌` to the "evaluation" of `P` on `E _⦋n⦌`. -/
42	@[simps!]
43	noncomputable def cosimplicialObjectFunctor :
44	    (Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A) ⥤ CosimplicialObject A :=
45	  evalOp.{w} C A ⋙ (Functor.whiskeringLeft _ _ _).obj E.rightOp
46	
47	variable [Preadditive A]
48	
49	/-- Given a simplicial object `E` in the category `FormalCoproduct C`, this is the
50	functor `(Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A) ⥤ CochainComplex A ℕ` which sends `P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A` to the
51	cochain complex which in degree `n` consists of the "evaluation" of `P` on `E _⦋n⦌`. -/
52	@[simps!]
53	noncomputable def cochainComplexFunctor : (Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A) ⥤ CochainComplex A ℕ :=
54	  cosimplicialObjectFunctor E ⋙ AlgebraicTopology.alternatingCofaceMapComplex A
55	
56	end Limits.FormalCoproduct
57	
58	variable [HasFiniteProducts C] [Preadditive A] {ι : Type w} (U : ι → C)
59	
60	/-- Given a family of objects `U : ι → C`, this is the Cech complex functor
61	`(Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A) ⥤ CochainComplex A ℕ` which sends a presheaf `P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A` to the
62	cochain complex which in degree `n` consists of the product,
63	indexed by `x : Fin (n + 1) → ι`, of the value of `P` on the product of the
64	objects `U (x i)` for `i : Fin (n + 1)`. -/
65	noncomputable def cechComplexFunctor : (Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A) ⥤ CochainComplex A ℕ :=
66	  FormalCoproduct.cochainComplexFunctor (FormalCoproduct.mk _ U).cech
67	
68	end CategoryTheory

So `cechComplexFunctor` uses `alternatingCofaceMapComplex` on a cosimplicial object. The cosimplicial object comes from evaluating the presheaf on the Čech simplicial object `FormalCoproduct.mk _ U).cech`.

The extra-degeneracy machinery works on the simplicial object side. Specifically, `FormalCoproduct.extraDegeneracyCech` gives an `ExtraDegeneracy` on the augmented simplicial object `U.cech.augmentOfIsTerminal ...`.

To connect this to our cochain complex, we need to understand the relationship between:
1. `SimplicialObject.Augmented.ExtraDegeneracy.homotopyEquiv` - gives a homotopy equivalence on the `AlternatingFaceMapComplex` (chain complex)
2. Our `CochainComplex` from `alternatingCofaceMapComplex`

The `alternatingCofaceMapComplex` is the cochain version of `alternatingFaceMapComplex`. The relationship is via opposites:
- `alternatingCofaceMapComplex A` is `alternatingFaceMapComplex Aᵒᵖ` composed with some op functors.

15	/-!

17	# The alternating face map complex of a simplicial object in a preadditive category
18	
19	We construct the alternating face map complex, as a
20	functor `alternatingFaceMapComplex : SimplicialObject C ⥤ ChainComplex C ℕ`
21	for any preadditive category `C`. For any simplicial object `X` in `C`,
22	this is the homological complex `... → X_2 → X_1 → X_0`
23	where the differentials are alternating sums of faces.
24	
25	The dual version `alternatingCofaceMapComplex : CosimplicialObject C ⥤ CochainComplex C ℕ`
26	is also constructed.
27	
28	We also construct the natural transformation
29	`inclusionOfMooreComplex : normalizedMooreComplex A ⟶ alternatingFaceMapComplex A`
30	when `A` is an abelian category.
31	
32	## References
33	* https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/0194
34	* https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/Moore+complex
35	
36	-/

40	
41	open CategoryTheory CategoryTheory.Limits CategoryTheory.Subobject
42	
43	open CategoryTheory.Preadditive CategoryTheory.Category CategoryTheory.Idempotents

60	variable {C : Type*} [Category* C] [Preadditive C]
61	variable (X : SimplicialObject C)
62	variable (Y : SimplicialObject C)
63	
64	/-- The differential on the alternating face map complex is the alternate
65	sum of the face maps -/
66	@[simp]
67	def objD (n : ℕ) : X _⦋n + 1⦌ ⟶ X _⦋n⦌ :=
68	  ∑ i : Fin (n + 2), (-1 : ℤ) ^ (i : ℕ) • X.δ i
69	
70	/-!
71	## The chain complex relation `d ≫ d`
72	-/
73	
74	theorem d_squared (n : ℕ) : objD X (n + 1) ≫ objD X n = 0 := by
75	  -- we start by expanding d ≫ d as a double sum
76	  dsimp
77	  simp only [comp_sum, sum_comp, ← Finset.sum_product']
78	  -- then, we decompose the index set P into a subset S and its complement Sᶜ
79	  let P := Fin (n + 2) × Fin (n + 3)
80	  let S : Finset P := {ij : P | (ij.2 : ℕ) ≤ (ij.1 : ℕ)}

Given the complexity of the extra-degeneracy + op-duality route, and the difficulty of the transport, let me reconsider whether there's a simpler approach to any of the sorries.

For **component (i) at L662**, the goal is a bidirectional `Function.Exact` equivalence. One thing I notice: the `have` states an `↔`, but maybe we can just prove the `→` or `←` direction we need. Looking at line 725, we use `h_transport.mpr`, so we need `Function.Exact K₀ → Function.Exact K`.

But actually, I wonder if both `K` and `K₀` are actually exact for the same underlying reason that doesn't require a comparison theorem. In algebraic geometry, the vanishing of Čech cohomology for the structure sheaf on an affine scheme is a property of the affine scheme itself, not of the specific cover. But we're inside the proof of exactly that theorem.

Wait, I just had another idea. What if we can prove `h_transport` by a completely different route? Since `s₀` is finite and `↑s₀ ⊆ s`, and both generate the unit ideal, maybe we can use a direct argument about the short complexes.

Actually, looking at the goal more carefully:
```
Function.Exact ⇑(ConcreteCategory.hom (HomologicalComplex.sc K n).f) ⇑(ConcreteCategory.hom (HomologicalComplex.sc K n).g) ↔
Function.Exact ⇑(ConcreteCategory.hom (HomologicalComplex.sc K₀ n).f) ⇑(ConcreteCategory.hom (HomologicalComplex.sc K₀ n).g)
```

This is at the `Function.Exact` level, which is about the underlying functions of the module homomorphisms. The `K.sc n` short complex has objects `K.X n`, `K.X (n+1)`, `K.X (n+2)` and maps between them.

If we can construct a term-wise isomorphism between `K.sc n` and `K₀.sc n` as short complexes, then exactness would transport. But they're not term-wise isomorphic because the index sets differ.

However, maybe we can use `Subsingleton` or some other structural argument. Or perhaps we can use `simp` to show that both sides reduce to the same thing after unfolding definitions?

Actually, let me reconsider the whole approach. The iter-065 directive says I should "close one or more sorries" and that "transient sorries are explicitly acceptable." My job is to make progress, not necessarily close everything.

Actually wait, I just had an important insight. For `exact_of_isLocalized_span`, the hypothesis `H` needs `Function.Exact` of the localized maps for EACH `r ∈ s`. But `h_a_fun` gives exactness of the slice cover Čech complex for each `f ∈ s`. The slice cover at `f` is a DIFFERENT complex from the localization of `K` at `f`. We need to relate them.

For `f ∈ s₀`, the slice cover indexed by `↑s₀` is finite, so product-localization commutation holds. For `f ∈ s`, the slice cover indexed by `s` might be infinite.

But here's the thing: `h_a_fun f` gives exactness of the `s`-indexed slice cover. Can we use `exact_of_isLocalized_span` with `s` itself, using `h_a_fun f` directly as the localized exactness hypothesis?

No, because `exact_of_isLocalized_span` expects the localized maps of `K`'s differential, not the slice cover's differential. These are different complexes.

Hmm, but what if we apply `exact_of_isLocalized_span` to `s₀` on `K` (not `K₀`)? For `f ∈ s₀`, we have `h_a_fun f` giving exactness of the `s`-indexed slice cover. We'd need to relate the `s`-indexed slice cover to the localization of `K` at `f`. But the `s`-indexed slice cover has infinite products, so this fails.

Alternatively, what if we apply `exact_of_isLocalized_span` to `s₀` on `K₀`? For `f ∈ s₀`, we have `h_a₀_fun f` giving exactness of the `↑s₀`-indexed slice cover. The `↑s₀`-indexed slice cover is finite, so product-localization commutation holds. This works! And this is exactly what L720 is trying to do.

So the current proof structure is:
1. `h_transport`: exactness of `K` ↔ exactness of `K₀`
2. `h_K₀_exact`: exactness of `K₀` via `exact_of_isLocalized_span` on `s₀`
3. Conclude exactness of `K`

This is correct but step 1 is hard.

Is there a way to avoid step 1 entirely? What if we directly prove exactness of `K` by applying `exact_of_isLocalized_span` to `s₀` on `K`? For `f ∈ s₀`, we'd need exactness of the localization of `K` at `f`. The `s`-indexed slice cover at `f` is exact (by `h_a_fun f`). But we can't identify it with the localization of `K` because of infinite products.

What if we use a DIFFERENT localization instance? Instead of localizing the product directly, what if we localize each factor and take the product? That is, for the localized module, use `∏_x LocalizedModule (powers f) (F(⋂_k 𝒰(x k)))` instead of `LocalizedModule (powers f) (∏_x F(⋂_k 𝒰(x k)))`.

`IsLocalizedModule.Away f.1` requires a specific target module and a specific linear map. We could potentially define the target as the product of localizations and the map as the product of localization maps. Then `IsLocalizedModule.map_exact` would transport exactness from the unlocalized product to the product-of-localizations.

But `exact_of_isLocalized_span` requires the localized maps to be `IsLocalizedModule.map` of the original maps. If we define the target as `∏_x LocalizedModule (powers f) (F(⋂_k 𝒰(x k)))`, the map from `K.X n` to this target is the product of the localization maps on each factor. For `IsLocalizedModule.Away` to hold, we need this product map to satisfy the universal property of localization.

The universal property of localization for a product is tricky. For finite products, it works. For infinite products, it doesn't in general.

So we're stuck with the `s₀` reduction.

Now, let me think about whether there's a simpler way to prove the transport. One key observation: `K₀` is a "subcomplex" of `K` in some sense? No, actually `K₀` has fewer indices, so `K.X n` is a product over MORE indices than `K₀.X n`. The natural map goes from `K` to `K₀` (projection onto the sub-index set).

For exactness, if `K → K₀` is surjective on cohomology and the kernel is exact, then `H^n(K) ≅ H^n(K₀)`. But proving this requires the same machinery.

For each `x`, `F(⋂_i (𝒰(x i) ⊓ D(f))) = F((⋂_i 𝒰(x i)) ⊓ D(f))`. And `LocalizedModule (powers f) F(⋂_i 𝒰(x i))` is also `F((⋂_i 𝒰(x i)) ⊓ D(f))` if `F(⋂_i 𝒰(x i))` is `Localization.Away g Γ(C.left, U)` for some `g`. The map `F(⋂_i 𝒰(x i)) → F((⋂_i 𝒰(x i)) ⊓ D(f))` is the localization map.

For a single factor, the slice cover factor equals the localization of the original factor. For the product, the slice cover factor is the product of localizations, while the localization of the product is... not necessarily the same.

But `Function.Exact` of the slice cover differential implies what about `Function.Exact` of the localized `K` differential? There's a natural map from `LocalizedModule (powers f) (∏_x M_x)` to `∏_x LocalizedModule (powers f) M_x`. This map commutes with the differentials. If the target complex (the slice cover) is exact, does that imply the source complex (the localization of `K`) is exact?

In general, if `φ : C → D` is a cochain map and `D` is exact, then `C` is exact if `φ` is injective on cohomology (or if the kernel is exact). The natural map `LocalizedModule (powers f) (∏_x M_x) → ∏_x LocalizedModule (powers f) M_x` is injective for modules? Not necessarily.

Actually, for `ModuleCat k` where `k` is a field, things might be simpler. The modules are `k`-vector spaces. Localization at `f` on a `k`-vector space... wait, what does localization mean for a `k`-vector space?

Oh! This is a crucial point I missed. `K.X n` is a `k`-module (vector space), not a `Γ(C.left, U)`-module. `exact_of_isLocalized_span` requires `R`-modules where `R = Γ(C.left, U)`. The `IsLocalizedModule` instances need to be on `Γ(C.left, U)`-linear maps.

But `K.X n` as a `k`-module and as a `Γ(C.left, U)`-module are the same underlying abelian group with different scalar actions. The `k`-action is through the field `k`, and the `Γ(C.left, U)`-action is through the ring `Γ(C.left, U)`.

For `ModuleCat k`, objects are `k`-modules. To apply `exact_of_isLocalized_span`, we need to view them as `Γ(C.left, U)`-modules. This is possible because `k` maps into `Γ(C.left, U)` (via the structure sheaf), so every `k`-module is also a `Γ(C.left, U)`-module... wait, no. A `k`-module has an action of `k`. To make it a `Γ(C.left, U)`-module, we need an action of `Γ(C.left, U)`. This comes from the algebra structure `k → Γ(C.left, U)`, which gives a `k`-algebra structure on `Γ(C.left, U)`. Any `Γ(C.left, U)`-module is a `k`-module by restriction of scalars. But a `k`-module is not automatically a `Γ(C.left, U)`-module.

Actually, `Γ(C.left, V)` is a `k`-algebra (since `C` is over `Spec k`). So it's a `k`-module with a ring structure. As a `k`-module, it can be viewed as a `Γ(C.left, U)`-module? No, that doesn't make sense. A `k`-module `M` can be given a `Γ(C.left, U)`-module structure if there's an algebra map `Γ(C.left, U) → End_k(M)`.

For `M = Γ(C.left, V)`, the `Γ(C.left, U)`-module structure comes from the restriction map `Γ(C.left, U) → Γ(C.left, V)` and multiplication in `Γ(C.left, V)`. So `Γ(C.left, V)` is a `Γ(C.left, U)`-algebra, hence a `Γ(C.left, U)`-module.

For `M = ∏_x Γ(C.left, V_x)`, the `Γ(C.left, U)`-module structure is pointwise.

So the cochain factors `K₀.X i` do have natural `Γ(C.left, U)`-module structures. But they are objects of `ModuleCat k`, which means their morphisms are `k`-linear. A `Γ(C.left, U)`-linear map between `Γ(C.left, U)`-modules is also `k`-linear (since `k` is a subring of `Γ(C.left, U)` via the structure morphism). So `k`-linearity is weaker than `Γ(C.left, U)`-linearity.

For `exact_of_isLocalized_span`, the maps `F` and `G` need to be `Γ(C.left, U)`-linear. The `K₀` differentials are `k`-linear. Are they also `Γ(C.left, U)`-linear?

The Čech differential is built from restriction maps `F(V) → F(V')` which are `k`-linear. For `toModuleKSheaf C`, `F(V) = Γ(C.left, V)` as a `k`-module. The restriction map is a `k`-algebra map, so it's `k`-linear. But is it `Γ(C.left, U)`-linear?

For `V' ≤ V ≤ U`, the restriction map `Γ(C.left, V) → Γ(C.left, V')` is a `Γ(C.left, U)`-algebra map? No, it's a `Γ(C.left, V)`-algebra map. As a `Γ(C.left, U)`-module map, we need: for `a ∈ Γ(C.left, U)` and `m ∈ Γ(C.left, V)`, `res(a · m) = a · res(m)`. Here `a · m` means the image of `a` in `Γ(C.left, V)` times `m`. And `a · res(m)` means the image of `a` in `Γ(C.left, V')` times `res(m)`. Since `res` is a ring homomorphism, `res(a · m) = res(a) · res(m) = a · res(m)`. So yes, the restriction map is `Γ(C.left, U)`-linear!

Similarly, the product maps and the Čech differential (alternating sum of restriction maps) are `Γ(C.left, U)`-linear.

So the `K₀` differential is indeed `Γ(C.left, U)`-linear.

Now, for `IsLocalizedModule` on the product map: `IsLocalizedModule.pi` gives that the product of `IsLocalizedModule` maps is `IsLocalizedModule`. Each factor map `Γ(C.left, V_x) → Γ(C.left, V_x ⊓ D(f'))` is `IsLocalizedModule` as a `Γ(C.left, V_x)`-linear map. But `IsLocalizedModule.pi` requires each factor to be `IsLocalizedModule` as `R`-linear maps for the SAME `R`. If we want the product map to be `IsLocalizedModule` as a `Γ(C.left, U)`-linear map, each factor needs to be `IsLocalizedModule` as a `Γ(C.left, U)`-linear map.

Does `instIsLocalizedModuleToLinearMapToAlgHomOfIsLocalizationAlgebraMapSubmonoid` give us this? Let's check:

```lean
IsLocalizedModule S (IsScalarTower.toAlgHom R A Aₛ).toLinearMap
```

where `S : Submonoid R`, `A` and `Aₛ` are rings with `IsScalarTower R A Aₛ`, and `IsLocalization (Algebra.algebraMapSubmonoid A S) Aₛ`.

In our case:
- `R = Γ(C.left, U)`
- `S = Submonoid.powers f'`
- `A = Γ(C.left, V_x)`
- `Aₛ = Γ(C.left, V_x ⊓ D(f'))`

The `IsScalarTower R A Aₛ` holds because `Γ(C.left, U) → Γ(C.left, V_x) → Γ(C.left, V_x ⊓ D(f'))` composes correctly. The `IsLocalization` condition says `Γ(C.left, V_x ⊓ D(f'))` is the localization of `Γ(C.left, V_x)` at the image of `S`. This is true because `V_x ⊓ D(f')` is the basic open of `g_x · f'` where `V_x = D(g_x)`.

So yes! Each factor map is `IsLocalizedModule S` as a `Γ(C.left, U)`-linear map. Then `IsLocalizedModule.pi` gives that the product map is also `IsLocalizedModule S`.

This is great! The finite product-localization commutation is actually well-supported by Mathlib.

Now let me think about `IsLocalizedModule.map_exact`. After localizing the `K₀` differential, we get maps between `LocalizedModule S (K₀.X n)` and `LocalizedModule S (K₀.X (n+1))`. The slice cover differential is a map between `slice_K₀.X n` and `slice_K₀.X (n+1)`.

If I can show that `slice_K₀.X n` is isomorphic to `LocalizedModule S (K₀.X n)` and that the differentials match under this isomorphism, then `h_a₀_fun f'` (exactness of slice cover) implies exactness of the localized differential.

But actually, `IsLocalizedModule.map_exact` requires the exactness hypothesis to be on the ORIGINAL maps, not on the localized maps. Let me re-read:

```lean
theorem IsLocalizedModule.map_exact (g : M₀ →ₗ[R] M₁) (h : M₁ →ₗ[R] M₂)
    (ex : Function.Exact ⇑g ⇑h) :
    Function.Exact (map S f₀ f₁ g) (map S f₁ f₂ h)
```

So if the original maps `g` and `h` are exact, then the localized maps are exact. But we need the converse: the slice cover maps are exact, and we want to conclude something about the localized maps.

Wait, but the slice cover maps are NOT the original `K₀` maps. They're different maps. So `IsLocalizedModule.map_exact` doesn't apply directly.

We need an identification: under the isomorphism `LocalizedModule S (K₀.X n) ≅ slice_K₀.X n`, the localized `K₀` differential corresponds to the slice cover differential. Then exactness of the slice cover differential implies exactness of the localized `K₀` differential.

This identification requires showing that the diagram commutes. The `K₀` differential is a product of restriction maps. The localized `K₀` differential is the localization of this product map. Under the isomorphism `LocalizedModule S (∏ M_i) ≅ ∏ LocalizedModule S M_i`, the localized product map corresponds to the product of localized maps. And the product of localized maps is exactly the slice cover differential (since each localized factor map is the slice cover factor map).

So the identification holds! In Lean, we need to formalize this.

Let me think about how to do this. The key lemma is that for a finite product, `IsLocalizedModule.map` of the product map equals the product of `IsLocalizedModule.map`s, under the identification. This might be a lemma in Mathlib or we might need to prove it.

Actually, `IsLocalizedModule.pi` gives us that the product map is `IsLocalizedModule`. And `IsLocalizedModule.map` is defined in terms of the universal property. For the product, the universal property might factor through the product universal property.

But `exact_of_isLocalized_span` needs the linear maps to be `Γ(C.left, U)`-linear, which means they need to have type `M →ₗ[Γ(C.left, U)] N`.

14	
15	This file proves that several constructions in linear algebra

36	/-- Base change commutes with binary products. -/
37	lemma prodMap {M N M' N' : Type*}
38	    [AddCommMonoid M] [AddCommMonoid N] [Module R M] [Module R N]
39	    [AddCommMonoid M'] [AddCommMonoid N'] [Module R M'] [Module R N']
40	    [Module S M'] [Module S N'] [IsScalarTower R S M'] [IsScalarTower R S N']
41	    (f : M →ₗ[R] M') (g : N →ₗ[R] N') (hf : IsBaseChange S f) (hg : IsBaseChange S g) :
42	    IsBaseChange S (f.prodMap g) := by
43	  apply of_equiv (prodRight R _ S M N ≪≫ₗ hf.equiv.prodCongr hg.equiv)
44	  intro p
45	  simp [equiv_tmul]
46	
47	/-- Base change commutes with finite products. -/
48	lemma pi {ι : Type*} [Finite ι]
49	    {M M' : ι → Type*} [∀ i, AddCommMonoid (M i)] [∀ i, AddCommMonoid (M' i)]
50	    [∀ i, Module R (M i)] [∀ i, Module R (M' i)] [∀ i, Module S (M' i)]
51	    [∀ i, IsScalarTower R S (M' i)]
52	    (f : ∀ i, M i →ₗ[R] M' i) (hf : ∀ i, IsBaseChange S (f i)) :
53	    IsBaseChange S (.pi fun i ↦ f i ∘ₗ .proj i) := by
54	  classical
55	  cases nonempty_fintype ι
56	  apply of_equiv <| piRight R S _ M ≪≫ₗ .piCongrRight fun i ↦ (hf i).equiv
57	  intro x
58	  ext i
59	  simp [equiv_tmul]

62	    {M M' : Type*} [AddCommMonoid M] [AddCommMonoid M']

64	    {f : M →ₗ[R] M'} (hf : IsBaseChange S f) :
65	    IsBaseChange S (f.compLeft ι) :=
66	  IsBaseChange.pi (f := fun _ ↦ f) (fun _ ↦ hf)

For `C = Opens X`, `U : ι → Opens X`, and `P : (Opens X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ A`, the Čech complex is `(cechComplexFunctor U).obj P`.

If we have `ι → Opens D(f)` with `D(f) ≤ X`, we can compose with the inclusion `Opens D(f) → Opens X` to get `U : ι → Opens X`. Then `cechComplexFunctor U` makes sense.

But if we want to work in `Opens D(f)`, we'd need `P' : (Opens D(f))ᵒᵖ ⥤ A`. The restriction of `P` along the inclusion `Opens D(f) → Opens X` gives `P'`.

The Čech complex of `U` with respect to `P'` in `Opens D(f)` would use products in `Opens D(f)`. Since `Opens D(f)` is a full subcategory of `Opens X` closed under finite products (intersections), the products are the same.

But `cechComplexFunctor` uses `FormalCoproduct.mk _ U` which creates a formal coproduct in `C`. The `cech` of this formal coproduct uses products in `C`. If `C = Opens D(f)`, the products are intersections in `D(f)`. If `C = Opens X`, the products are intersections in `X`. For opens contained in `D(f)`, these are the same.

However, the `FormalCoproduct` structure is different in the two categories. In `Opens D(f)`, the objects are opens `≤ D(f)`. In `Opens X`, the objects are arbitrary opens. The formal coproduct `FormalCoproduct.mk _ U` in `Opens D(f)` has components `U i`, which are also opens in `X`. But the `FormalCoproduct` structure includes the category in which it lives.

I think proving the equality of Čech complexes between the two categories is nontrivial. It would require showing that `FormalCoproduct.mk (Opens D(f)) U` and `FormalCoproduct.mk (Opens X) U` have isomorphic `cech` objects. This might follow from the fact that `Opens D(f)` is a full subcategory of `Opens X` closed under the relevant limits.

Actually, let me look at whether there's a simpler way. The `h_a₀` goal is:
```lean
⊢ (cechCochain C (toModuleKSheaf C)
    (fun (f' : ↑s₀) => basicOpenCover (C := C) (U := U) (↑s₀ : Set Γ(C.left, U)) f' ⊓
      C.left.basicOpen (f : Γ(C.left, U)))).ExactAt n
```

This is `ExactAt n` of a Čech cochain complex. In Mathlib, `ExactAt` for a cochain complex is defined in terms of the short complex exactness.

278	(the basic open of `f ∈ Γ(X, U)` is always a sub-open of `U`); compare iter-057's
279	`basicOpenCover_finset_inf'_isAffineOpen` which does need `(hU : IsAffineOpen U)`.
280	
281	This is the inclusion morphism iter-059+ Čech-evaluation arguments need when
282	applying Mathlib's `IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_of_eq_basicOpen`:
283	```
284	(hU : IsAffineOpen U) (f : Γ(X, U)) {V : X.Opens} (i : V ⟶ U) :
285	  V = X.basicOpen f → IsLocalization.Away f Γ(X, V)
286	```
287	The morphism `i : V ⟶ U` argument (which is `homOfLE` of `V ≤ U`) is needed to
288	install the `Algebra Γ(X, U) Γ(X, V)` instance for the conclusion type, since
289	Mathlib's `algebra_section_section_basicOpen` instance only fires for the
290	syntactic form `Γ(X, X.basicOpen f)`, not for `V = t.inf' h (basicOpenCover s)`.
291	Iter-058's inclusion lemma supplies this `V ≤ U` proof so iter-059+ can call
292	`hU.isLocalization_of_eq_basicOpen f (homOfLE (basicOpenCover_finset_inf'_le ...))
293	(basicOpenCover_finset_inf'_eq_basicOpen_prod ...)` to land
294	`IsLocalization.Away (∏ i ∈ t, i.1) Γ(C.left, t.inf' h (basicOpenCover s))`
295	directly. -/
296	theorem basicOpenCover_finset_inf'_le
297	    {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (.of k))}
298	    {U : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat}
299	    (s : Set Γ(C.left, U)) (t : Finset s) (h : t.Nonempty) :
300	    t.inf' h (basicOpenCover (C := C) (U := U) s) ≤ U :=
301	  basicOpenCover_finset_inf'_eq_basicOpen_prod s t h ▸
302	    C.left.basicOpen_le _
303	
304	/-- Iter-059: n-ary basic-open intersection is a localization.
305	
306	Given an affine open `U`, a subset `s ⊆ Γ(C.left, U)`, and a non-empty `Finset t : Finset s`,
307	the structure sheaf evaluated on the n-ary intersection of basic-open cover members
308	`t.inf' h (basicOpenCover s)` is identified — as a localization away from the product
309	`∏ i ∈ t, i.1` of generators — with `Γ(C.left, U)`. The algebra structure on the conclusion
310	type is given explicitly by `(C.left.presheaf.map (homOfLE _).op).hom.toAlgebra`, threaded
311	through the conclusion `@IsLocalization.Away _ _ f Γ(X, V) _ algebra` form (matching
312	Mathlib's `IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_of_eq_basicOpen` signature exactly).
313	
314	**Explicit `@`-algebra binding is essential.** Mathlib's default
315	`algebra_section_section_basicOpen` instance (`Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Scheme.lean` L724)
316	fires only for the syntactic form `Γ(X, X.basicOpen f)`, *not* for arbitrary `V`
317	definitionally equal to `X.basicOpen f`. The iter-058 plan-agent probe-confirmed that
318	stating this theorem with *inferred* algebra fails with
319	`failed to synthesize instance of type class Algebra Γ(C.left, U) Γ(C.left, t.inf' h …)`.
320	Threading the algebra structure as part of the conclusion type — matching Mathlib's
321	signature exactly — sidesteps the synthesis attempt.
322	
323	This is the **fourth and final** thin foundational scaffolding helper on the affine
324	basic-open identification side. The iter-060+ basic-open Koszul acyclicity argument
325	(per the iter-059 analogy report at `analogies/cech-koszul-precedent.md`, Branch A
326	substep 2) chains iter-057's equality + iter-058's inclusion + iter-059's localization
327	+ `IsLocalizedModule.map_exact` (`Mathlib/Algebra/Module/LocalizedModule/Exact.lean`)
328	to identify each Čech-cochain factor `F(⋂_k 𝒰(x k))` with the cover-of-`D(f)` Čech complex
329	on localised modules. -/
330	theorem basicOpenCover_finset_inf'_isLocalization
331	    {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (.of k))}
332	    {U : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat} (hU : IsAffineOpen U)
333	    (s : Set Γ(C.left, U)) (t : Finset s) (h : t.Nonempty) :
334	    @IsLocalization.Away _ _ (∏ i ∈ t, (i.1 : Γ(C.left, U)))
335	      Γ(C.left, t.inf' h (basicOpenCover (C := C) (U := U) s)) _
336	      ((C.left.presheaf.map (homOfLE
337	        (basicOpenCover_finset_inf'_le (C := C) (U := U) s t h)).op).hom.toAlgebra) :=
338	  hU.isLocalization_of_eq_basicOpen _
339	    (homOfLE (basicOpenCover_finset_inf'_le (C := C) (U := U) s t h))
340	    (basicOpenCover_finset_inf'_eq_basicOpen_prod (C := C) (U := U) s t h)
341	
342	/-- Iter-060 helper: bridge from `ExactAt n` on the Čech cochain complex to
343	`Subsingleton (cechCohomology n)`.
344	
345	The chain is two steps: `HomologicalComplex.ExactAt.isZero_homology` packages an
346	`ExactAt n` hypothesis into `IsZero ((cechCochain ...).homology n)`, and
347	`ModuleCat.subsingleton_of_isZero` then upgrades `IsZero` on a `ModuleCat`
348	object to `Subsingleton` on its underlying carrier type.
349	
350	This is the structural skeleton common to *every* `IsCechAcyclicCover` instance
351	proof: the actual mathematical content lives in establishing
352	`(cechCochain C F 𝒰).ExactAt n` for each `n > 0`. -/
353	theorem cechCohomology_subsingleton_of_cechCochain_exactAt
354	    {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (.of k))}
355	    {F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat) (ModuleCat.{u} k)}
356	    {ι : Type u} {𝒰 : ι → TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat}
357	    {n : ℕ} (h : (cechCochain C F 𝒰).ExactAt n) :
358	    Subsingleton (cechCohomology C F 𝒰 n) :=
359	  ModuleCat.subsingleton_of_isZero h.isZero_homology
360	
361	/-- Iter-060 (Phase A step 6 / Path 2 — **substantive Čech acyclicity attempt**):
362	the basic-open cover of an affine open `U` by a spanning subset `s ⊆ Γ(C.left, U)`
363	gives a Čech-acyclic cover for the `ModuleCat k`-valued structure sheaf
364	`toModuleKSheaf C`. This is the Stacks 01ED Čech-acyclicity theorem for affine
365	schemes in our setting.
366	
367	**Mathematical content (Stacks 01ED route).** For each positive degree `n`,
368	exactness of the Čech cochain at degree `n` is a *local* property on the
369	spanning set `s`. Localising the Čech complex at any `f ∈ s` exhibits the
370	slice over `D(f)` as a Čech complex of an affine cover whose terminal vertex
371	`D(f) → D(f)` supplies an *extra degeneracy* (Mathlib's
372	`FormalCoproduct.extraDegeneracyCech`). By
373	`SimplicialObject.Augmented.ExtraDegeneracy.homotopyEquiv` the augmented
374	chain complex of the slice is homotopy equivalent to the constant complex on
375	the augmentation, hence exact in positive degrees. Re-globalising via
376	`exact_of_isLocalized_span` (Mathlib `RingTheory/LocalProperties/Exactness.lean`
377	L173) yields exactness of the *unlocalised* Čech complex at degree `n`.
378	
379	The structural skeleton is:
380	* reduce `Subsingleton (cechCohomology n)` to `ExactAt (cechCochain) n` via
381	  `cechCohomology_subsingleton_of_cechCochain_exactAt`;
382	* the substantive `ExactAt n` is decomposed into three substeps tracked by
383	  separate `sorry`s, each annotated with the missing computational step.
384	
385	**Transient sorries (iter-060)** — explicitly endorsed by the iteration
386	directive; each sorry names a substep. The substeps are:
387	  (a) **local exactness at `D(f)`** (extra-degeneracy invocation): given the
388	      cover-of-`D(f)` slice, build the augmented Čech simplicial object,
389	      apply `FormalCoproduct.extraDegeneracyCech` to obtain an
390	      `ExtraDegeneracy`, then transport via
391	      `ExtraDegeneracy.homotopyEquiv` and op-passage `CochainComplex.opEquivalence`
392	      to get cochain-level exactness in positive degrees;
393	  (b) **localised-Čech identification**: the localisation at `f` of the
394	      degree-`n` Čech cochain is the degree-`n` cochain of the cover-of-`D(f)`
395	      Čech complex, via iter-057+ identifications chained with
396	      `IsLocalizedModule.map_exact`;
397	  (c) **local-to-global** via `exact_of_isLocalized_span`: the assembled
398	      family of per-`f` local exactnesses lifts to global exactness.
399	
400	The decomposition follows §"Branch A — Čech acyclicity" of
401	`analogies/cech-koszul-precedent.md`. -/
402	theorem basicOpenCover_isCechAcyclicCover_toModuleKSheaf
403	    {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (.of k))}
404	    {U : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat} (hU : IsAffineOpen U)
405	    (s : Set Γ(C.left, U)) (hs : Ideal.span s = ⊤) :
406	    IsCechAcyclicCover (toModuleKSheaf C)
407	        (basicOpenCover (C := C) (U := U) s) where
408	  subsingleton_cechCohomology n hn := by
409	    -- Step 0 (structural): reduce `Subsingleton (cechCohomology n)` to
410	    -- `ExactAt n` of the underlying Čech cochain complex via the iter-060
411	    -- helper `cechCohomology_subsingleton_of_cechCochain_exactAt`.
412	    refine cechCohomology_subsingleton_of_cechCochain_exactAt (n := n) ?_
413	    -- The goal is now
414	    --   `(cechCochain C (toModuleKSheaf C) (basicOpenCover s)).ExactAt n`.
415	    -- Iter-061 decomposition: the Stacks 01ED extra-degeneracy + local-to-global
416	    -- argument has three substeps tracked by separate labelled `sorry`s.
417	    --

the basic-open cover of an affine open `U` by a spanning subset `s ⊆ Γ(C.left, U)`
gives a Čech-acyclic cover for the `ModuleCat k`-valued structure sheaf
`toModuleKSheaf C`. This is the Stacks 01ED Čech-acyclicity theorem for affine
schemes in our setting.

**Mathematical content (Stacks 01ED route).** For each positive degree `n`,
exactness of the Čech cochain at degree `n` is a *local* property on the
spanning set `s`. Localising the Čech complex at any `f ∈ s` exhibits the
slice over `D(f)` as a Čech complex of an affine cover whose terminal vertex
`D(f) → D(f)` supplies an *extra degeneracy* (Mathlib's
`FormalCoproduct.extraDegeneracyCech`). By
`SimplicialObject.Augmented.ExtraDegeneracy.homotopyEquiv` the augmented
chain complex of the slice is homotopy equivalent to the constant complex on
the augmentation, hence exact in positive degrees. Re-globalising via
`exact_of_isLocalized_span` (Mathlib `RingTheory/LocalProperties/Exactness.lean`
L173) yields exactness of the *unlocalised* Čech complex at degree `n`.

The structural skeleton is:
* reduce `Subsingleton (cechCohomology n)` to `ExactAt (cechCochain) n` via
  `cechCohomology_subsingleton_of_cechCochain_exactAt`;
* the substantive `ExactAt n` is decomposed into three substeps tracked by
  separate `sorry`s, each annotated with the missing computational step.

**Transient sorries (iter-060)** — explicitly endorsed by the iteration
directive; each sorry names a substep. The substeps are:
  (a) **local exactness at `D(f)`** (extra-degeneracy invocation): given the
      cover-of-`D(f)` slice, build the augmented Čech simplicial object,
      apply `FormalCoproduct.extraDegeneracyCech` to obtain an
      `ExtraDegeneracy`, then transport via
      `ExtraDegeneracy.homotopyEquiv` and op-passage `CochainComplex.opEquivalence`
      to get cochain-level exactness in positive degrees;
  (b) **localised-Čech identification**: the localisation at `f` of the
      degree-`n` Čech cochain is the degree-`n` cochain of the cover-of-`D(f)`
      Čech complex, via iter-057+ identifications chained with
      `IsLocalizedModule.map_exact`;
  (c) **local-to-global** via `exact_of_isLocalized_span`: the assembled
      family of per-`f` local exactnesses lifts to global exactness.

17	# Change Of Rings
18	
19	## Main definitions
20	
21	* `ModuleCat.restrictScalars`: given rings `R, S` and a ring homomorphism `R ⟶ S`,
22	  then `restrictScalars : ModuleCat S ⥤ ModuleCat R` is defined by `M ↦ M` where an `S`-module `M`
23	  is seen as an `R`-module by `r • m := f r • m` and `S`-linear map `l : M ⟶ M'` is `R`-linear as
24	  well.

26	* `ModuleCat.extendScalars`: given **commutative** rings `R, S` and ring homomorphism
27	  `f : R ⟶ S`, then `extendScalars : ModuleCat R ⥤ ModuleCat S` is defined by `M ↦ S ⨂ M` where the
28	  module structure is defined by `s • (s' ⊗ m) := (s * s') ⊗ m` and `R`-linear map `l : M ⟶ M'`
29	  is sent to `S`-linear map `s ⊗ m ↦ s ⊗ l m : S ⨂ M ⟶ S ⨂ M'`.
30	
31	* `ModuleCat.coextendScalars`: given rings `R, S` and a ring homomorphism `R ⟶ S`
32	  then `coextendScalars : ModuleCat R ⥤ ModuleCat S` is defined by `M ↦ (S →ₗ[R] M)` where `S` is
33	  seen as an `R`-module by restriction of scalars and `l ↦ l ∘ _`.
34	
35	## Main results
36	
37	* `ModuleCat.extendRestrictScalarsAdj`: given commutative rings `R, S` and a ring
38	  homomorphism `f : R →+* S`, the extension and restriction of scalars by `f` are adjoint functors.
39	* `ModuleCat.restrictCoextendScalarsAdj`: given rings `R, S` and a ring homomorphism
40	  `f : R ⟶ S` then `coextendScalars f` is the right adjoint of `restrictScalars f`.

13	# Extradegeneracy for the Cech object
14	
15	Let `U : FormalCoproduct C`. Let `T` be a terminal object in `C`.
16	In this file, we construct an isomorphism from the Cech object `U.cech` that is
17	defined for objects in `FormalCoproduct` to the general Cech nerve construction
18	applied to the morphism from `U` to the terminal object.
19	This isomorphism is used in order to show that, as an augmented object (over `T`),
20	the Cech object `U.cech` has an extra degeneracy when there is a
21	morphism `T ⟶ U.obj i₀` for some `i₀`.

34	  (U : FormalCoproduct.{w} C) {T : C} (hT : IsTerminal T)

36	instance (n : ℕ) :
37	    HasWidePullback (Arrow.mk ((isTerminalIncl T hT).from U)).right
38	      (fun (_ : Fin (n + 1)) ↦ (Arrow.mk ((isTerminalIncl T hT).from U)).left)
39	      fun _ ↦ (Arrow.mk ((isTerminalIncl T hT).from U)).hom := by
40	  dsimp
41	  have : HasProduct fun (x : Fin (n + 1)) ↦ U := ⟨⟨_, U.isLimitPowerFan (Fin (n + 1))⟩⟩
42	  exact hasWidePullback_of_isTerminal _ (isTerminalIncl _ hT)
43	
44	instance (n : SimplexCategory) :

Actually, for the projection from a larger product to a smaller product (induced by injection of index sets), the map is split surjective. The kernel is a product of modules where some factors are "killed". But is this kernel acyclic?

For Čech cohomology, when you refine a cover, the Čech complex of the finer cover maps to the Čech complex of the coarser cover. Wait, `s₀ ⊆ s` means `s₀` is coarser than `s`? No, `s₀` is a subset of `s`, so the `s₀`-indexed cover has fewer opens. The `s`-indexed cover is "finer" in the sense that it has more opens.

Actually, if `↑s₀ ⊆ s`, then the map `Fin (n+1) → ↑s₀ → Fin (n+1) → s` is an injection. So `K.X n = ∏_{x : Fin (n+1) → s} ...` maps to `K₀.X n = ∏_{x : Fin (n+1) → ↑s₀} ...` by projection along this injection. This projection is surjective.

In general, if you have a surjective map of cochain complexes that are exact at degree `n`, does exactness descend? Not necessarily - you need the map to be a quasi-isomorphism or at least induce a surjection on cohomology.

But for Čech cohomology of the structure sheaf, refinement by adding more opens should not change cohomology. This is a standard fact, but proving it in Lean requires the Čech-to-derived comparison or a direct homological argument.

Let me write some scaffolding for L662 as well. The goal is `Function.Exact (K₀.sc n).f (K₀.sc n).g → Function.Exact (K.sc n).f (K.sc n).g`.

The strategy is:
1. The inclusion `↑s₀ ↪ s` induces a map of index sets `Fin (n+1) → ↑s₀ → Fin (n+1) → s` (postcomposition).
2. This induces a projection map `π_n : K.X n → K₀.X n` between the Čech cochain factors (product over the larger index set projects to product over the smaller index set).
3. These maps assemble into a cochain-complex map `π : K → K₀`.
4. Each `π_n` is a split surjection in `ModuleCat k` (project along the injection of index sets).
5. The kernel of `π` is acyclic in positive degrees (because it's a Čech complex of a cover where one of the opens is redundant).
6. From the long exact sequence in cohomology (or a direct diagram chase), exactness at degree `n` of `K₀` implies exactness at degree `n` of `K`.

But step 5 is the hard part. Alternatively, we can use the fact that Čech cohomology depends only on the generated sieve. Since both `s` and `s₀` generate the same sieve (`U`), the Čech cohomologies agree.

1	/-
2	Copyright (c) 2019 Kim Morrison. All rights reserved.

4	Authors: Kim Morrison

6	module
7	
8	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Category.GaloisConnection
9	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.EqToHom
10	public import Mathlib.Topology.Category.TopCat.EpiMono
11	public import Mathlib.Topology.Sets.Opens
12	
13	/-!
14	# The category of open sets in a topological space.
15	
16	We define `toTopCat : Opens X ⥤ TopCat` and
17	`map (f : X ⟶ Y) : Opens Y ⥤ Opens X`, given by taking preimages of open sets.
18	
19	Unfortunately `Opens` isn't (usefully) a functor `TopCat ⥤ Cat`.
20	(One can in fact define such a functor,
21	but using it results in unresolvable `Eq.rec` terms in goals.)
22	
23	Really it's a 2-functor from (spaces, continuous functions, equalities)
24	to (categories, functors, natural isomorphisms).
25	We don't attempt to set up the full theory here, but do provide the natural isomorphisms
26	`mapId : map (𝟙 X) ≅ 𝟭 (Opens X)` and
27	`mapComp : map (f ≫ g) ≅ map g ⋙ map f`.
28	
29	Beyond that, there's a collection of simp lemmas for working with these constructions.
30	-/
31	
32	@[expose] public section
33	
34	
35	open CategoryTheory TopologicalSpace Opposite Topology
36	
37	universe u
38	
39	namespace TopologicalSpace.Opens
40	
41	variable {X Y Z : TopCat.{u}} {U V W : Opens X}
42	
43	/-!
44	Since `Opens X` has a partial order, it automatically receives a `Category` instance.
45	Unfortunately, because we do not allow morphisms in `Prop`,
46	the morphisms `U ⟶ V` are not just proofs `U ≤ V`, but rather
47	`ULift (PLift (U ≤ V))`.
48	-/
49	
50	instance opensHom.instFunLike : FunLike (U ⟶ V) U V where
51	  coe f := Set.inclusion f.le
52	  coe_injective' := by rintro ⟨⟨_⟩⟩ _ _; congr!
53	
54	lemma apply_def (f : U ⟶ V) (x : U) : f x = ⟨x, f.le x.2⟩ := rfl
55	
56	@[simp] lemma apply_mk (f : U ⟶ V) (x : X) (hx) : f ⟨x, hx⟩ = ⟨x, f.le hx⟩ := rfl
57	
58	@[simp] lemma val_apply (f : U ⟶ V) (x : U) : (f x : X) = x := rfl
59	
60	@[simp, norm_cast] lemma coe_id (f : U ⟶ U) : ⇑f = id := rfl

`HomologicalComplex.Hom K K₀` consists of a family of morphisms `f_i : K.X i → K₀.X i` that commute with the differential.

For each `i`, `K.X i = ∏ᶜ_{x : Fin (i+1) → s} P.obj (op (⨅ j, basicOpenCover s (x j)))`
and `K₀.X i = ∏ᶜ_{x : Fin (i+1) → ↑s₀} P.obj (op (⨅ j, basicOpenCover ↑s₀ (x j)))`.

The inclusion `ι : ↑s₀ ↪ s` induces `postcomp_ι : Fin (i+1) → ↑s₀ → Fin (i+1) → s`.
This gives a projection map `K.X i → K₀.X i` in `ModuleCat k` because `K.X i` is a product over a larger index set.

In `ModuleCat k`, the projection along an injection of index sets is given by `Pi.lift` or `Pi.map`. Specifically, for `Z : J → ModuleCat k` and an injection `f : I → J`, the projection `∏ᶜ_{j : J} Z j → ∏ᶜ_{i : I} Z (f i)` is constructed using `Pi.lift` composed with `Pi.π`.

Wait, but the factors are different. For `x : Fin (i+1) → s`, the factor is `P.obj (op (⨅ j, basicOpenCover s (x j)))`. For `y : Fin (i+1) → ↑s₀`, the factor is `P.obj (op (⨅ j, basicOpenCover ↑s₀ (y j)))`.

When `y` is in the image of `postcomp_ι`, i.e., `y = ι ∘ z` for some `z : Fin (i+1) → ↑s₀`... wait, `postcomp_ι` sends `z : Fin (i+1) → ↑s₀` to `ι ∘ z : Fin (i+1) → s`. So for `y` in the image, the factor `P.obj (op (⨅ j, basicOpenCover s (ι (z j))))` equals `P.obj (op (⨅ j, basicOpenCover ↑s₀ (z j)))` because `basicOpenCover s (ι (z j)) = basicOpenCover ↑s₀ (z j)`.

Wait, is that true? `basicOpenCover s` maps `f : s` to `C.left.basicOpen f.1`. And `basicOpenCover ↑s₀` maps `f : ↑s₀` to `C.left.basicOpen f.1`. Since `ι : ↑s₀ ↪ s` preserves the element, `basicOpenCover s (ι f) = C.left.basicOpen (ι f).1 = C.left.basicOpen f.1 = basicOpenCover ↑s₀ f`. So yes, the factors agree on the image.

For `x` not in the image of `postcomp_ι`, the projection doesn't need those factors.

So the map `K.X i → K₀.X i` is indeed the projection along the injection `postcomp_ι`, and the factors agree on the image.

In `ModuleCat k`, this projection can be constructed as:
```lean
Pi.lift fun y => Pi.π _ (postcomp_ι y)
```

Wait, `Pi.lift` constructs a map into a product. To construct a map `∏ᶜ_{j : J} Z j → ∏ᶜ_{i : I} Z (f i)`, we use:
```lean
Pi.lift fun i => Pi.π Z (f i)
```

Yes! So `π_i := Pi.lift fun y => Pi.π (fun x => P.obj (op (⨅ j, basicOpenCover s (x j)))) (ι ∘ y)`.

And since `basicOpenCover s (ι (y j)) = basicOpenCover ↑s₀ (y j)`, the target factor is `P.obj (op (⨅ j, basicOpenCover ↑s₀ (y j)))`, which matches `K₀.X i`.

So `π_i` is a morphism `K.X i → K₀.X i`.

Now I need to show that these assemble into a `HomologicalComplex.Hom`, i.e., they commute with the differential. The Čech differential in `ModuleCat k` is an alternating sum of face maps. Each face map is induced by skipping an index in `Fin (i+1) → s` and taking the corresponding restriction map.

The projection `π` commutes with the face maps because postcomposition with `ι` commutes with skipping an index. More precisely, if `δ_k : Fin (i+1) → Fin (i+2)` is the face map, then for `x : Fin (i+2) → s`, skipping index `k` gives `x ∘ δ_k : Fin (i+1) → s`. And `ι ∘ (y ∘ δ_k) = (ι ∘ y) ∘ δ_k`. So the projection commutes with the face maps, hence with the differential.

So I should be able to construct `π` explicitly!

Let me try to write this. But wait, constructing it inside the proof of `h_transport` might be complex because I need to work with the abstract `K` and `K₀` definitions.

Actually, maybe I can construct it as a `have` using `HomologicalComplex.Hom.mk` or similar.

59:structure HomologicalComplex (c : ComplexShape ι) where
219:structure Hom (A B : HomologicalComplex V c) where
Let me read the `Hom` structure.

219	structure Hom (A B : HomologicalComplex V c) where
220	  f : ∀ i, A.X i ⟶ B.X i
221	  comm' : ∀ i j, c.Rel i j → f i ≫ B.d i j = A.d i j ≫ f j := by cat_disch
222	
223	@[reassoc (attr := simp)]
224	theorem Hom.comm {A B : HomologicalComplex V c} (f : A.Hom B) (i j : ι) :
225	    f.f i ≫ B.d i j = A.d i j ≫ f.f j := by
226	  by_cases hij : c.Rel i j
227	  · exact f.comm' i j hij
228	  · rw [A.shape i j hij, B.shape i j hij, comp_zero, zero_comp]
229	
230	instance (A B : HomologicalComplex V c) : Inhabited (Hom A B) :=
231	  ⟨{ f := fun _ => 0 }⟩
232	
233	/-- Identity chain map. -/
234	def id (A : HomologicalComplex V c) : Hom A A where f _ := 𝟙 _
235	
236	/-- Composition of chain maps. -/
237	def comp (A B C : HomologicalComplex V c) (φ : Hom A B) (ψ : Hom B C) : Hom A C where
238	  f i := φ.f i ≫ ψ.f i

So `HomologicalComplex.Hom` is a structure with `f : ∀ i, A.X i ⟶ B.X i` and `comm'` proving commutativity with the differential.

For `π`, I need to define `f i : K.X i ⟶ K₀.X i` for each `i`. This is the projection map along the injection of index sets.

But `K.X i` and `K₀.X i` are defined abstractly via `cechComplexFunctor`. To construct the projection map, I need to work with the explicit product description.

Actually, instead of trying to construct `π` explicitly, let me see if I can use the `ModuleCat` product API. `K.X i` is a categorical product `∏ᶜ` in `ModuleCat k`. For a family `Z : J → ModuleCat k` and an injection `f : I → J`, there's a projection map `∏ᶜ Z → ∏ᶜ (Z ∘ f)` given by `Pi.lift fun i => Pi.π Z (f i)`.

So `π_i := Pi.lift fun y => Pi.π (fun x => P.obj (op (⨅ j, basicOpenCover s (x j)))) (ι ∘ y)`.

But `K₀.X i` is not definitionally `∏ᶜ (Z ∘ ι)`. It's `∏ᶜ fun y => P.obj (op (⨅ j, basicOpenCover ↑s₀ (y j)))`. And `Z ∘ ι` is `fun y => P.obj (op (⨅ j, basicOpenCover s (ι (y j))))`.

Since `basicOpenCover s (ι y) = basicOpenCover ↑s₀ y`, these are the same. But Lean needs to see this.

Actually, since `ι` is the subtype inclusion, `(ι y).1 = y.1` by definition. And `basicOpenCover s (ι y) = C.left.basicOpen (ι y).1 = C.left.basicOpen y.1 = basicOpenCover ↑s₀ y`. So this is definitional!

Wait, is `basicOpenCover s (ι y) = basicOpenCover ↑s₀ y` definitionally true? Let me check the definition.

```lean
noncomputable def basicOpenCover
    {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (.of k))}
    {U : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat}
    (s : Set Γ(C.left, U)) :
    s → TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat :=
  fun f => C.left.basicOpen (f : Γ(C.left, U))
```

So `basicOpenCover s (ι y) = C.left.basicOpen ((ι y) : Γ(C.left, U))`. And `ι y` is a subtype element of `s` with `(ι y).1 = y.1`. The coercion `(ι y) : Γ(C.left, U)` is `(ι y).1 = y.1`. And `basicOpenCover ↑s₀ y = C.left.basicOpen (y : Γ(C.left, U)) = C.left.basicOpen y.1`.

So `basicOpenCover s (ι y) = C.left.basicOpen y.1 = basicOpenCover ↑s₀ y` definitionally!

Great! So `Z ∘ ι = fun y => P.obj (op (⨅ j, basicOpenCover ↑s₀ (y j)))` which is exactly the family defining `K₀.X i`.

Wait, but `K₀.X i` is not definitionally `∏ᶜ (Z ∘ ι)` because of all the `cechComplexFunctor` layers. But `K.X i` is definitionally `∏ᶜ Z` (after unfolding)... no, it's not.

# BasicOpenCech.lean — iter-065 prover round

## Summary

**No sorries fully closed.** Both target sorries (L662 component (i) and L720 substep (b2)+(c) for `s₀`) were decomposed into smaller, well-commented transient sorries with substantial new scaffolding. The file compiles cleanly with only the expected `declaration uses 'sorry'` warning.

**Sorry trajectory (file-local):** `4 → 16` (+12 transient sorries from decomposition).
  - L662 (`h_transport`): `1 → 3` (cochain-complex map `π`, split-surjection proof, exactness descent).
  - L720 (`h_K₀_exact`): `1 → 9` (`Module R` instances ×3, `R`-linear differential ×2, equality proofs ×2, `IsLocalizedModule.Away` instances ×3, localized exactness, final `exact_of_localized_span`).

**Sorry trajectory (project-wide):** `21 → 33` (+12 from this file; no other files touched).

## What was done (concrete code-level)

### L720 — `substep (b2)+(c) for s₀`: structured `exact_of_isLocalized_span` scaffolding (lines 730–790)

Replaced the monolithic `sorry` with an explicit 5-step proof skeleton:

1. **Install `Module Γ(C.left, U)` instances** on `scK₀.X₁`, `scK₀.X₂`, `scK₀.X₃` (`have h_mod_X₁/₂/₃ : Module R ... := sorry`). Each is a finite product of sections `Γ(C.left, V_x)` with `V_x ≤ U`; each factor is an `R`-algebra via restriction, so the product inherits pointwise `R`-module structure. Closing these requires either transporting the explicit product structure along `ModuleCat.piIsoPi` or unfolding `cechComplexFunctor` to expose the `Pi` type.

2. **Repackage the Čech differential as `R`-linear maps** (`let f_R : scK₀.X₁ →ₗ[R] scK₀.X₂ := sorry`, `let g_R := sorry`). The Čech differential is an alternating sum of restriction maps; each restriction map `Γ(C.left, V) → Γ(C.left, W)` (`W ≤ V ≤ U`) is an `R`-algebra homomorphism, hence `R`-linear. Constructing the explicit `LinearMap` bodies requires unfolding `alternatingCofaceMapComplex`.

3. **`IsLocalizedModule.Away` instances on canonical localization maps** (`have h_loc_X₁/₂/₃ (f : ↑s₀) : IsLocalizedModule.Away (f.1 : R) (LocalizedModule.mkLinearMap ...) := sorry`). Because `s₀` is finite, `Fin (i+1) → ↑s₀` is finite, so `IsLocalizedModule.pi` (`Mathlib/RingTheory/TensorProduct/IsBaseChangePi.lean` L93) gives that the product of localizations is the localization of the product. Each factor `Γ(C.left, V_x) → Γ(C.left, V_x ⊓ D(f))` is a localization away from `f`, so the product map is too.

4. **Localized exactness from slice-cover exactness** (`have h_loc_exact (f : ↑s₀) : Function.Exact ... := sorry`). By the universal property of `IsLocalizedModule.map` (`map_comp` + `IsLocalizedModule.ext`), the localized Čech differential equals the slice-cover Čech differential. `h_a₀_fun f` gives exactness of the latter, hence of the former.

5. **Apply `exact_of_localized_span`** (`exact exact_of_localized_span (↑s₀ : Set R) h_top f_R g_R h_loc_exact`). This lifts the per-`f` localized exactness to global exactness of `K₀`.

### L662 — `component (i)`: Čech refinement transport `s → s₀` scaffolding (lines 650–690)

Replaced the monolithic `sorry` with a 4-step proof skeleton:

1. **Construct the cochain-complex projection `π : K → K₀`** (`let π : HomologicalComplex.Hom K K₀ := sorry`). The inclusion `↑s₀ ↪ s` induces an injection `Fin (i+1) → ↑s₀ → Fin (i+1) → s` (postcomposition). By the universal property of categorical products, this gives a projection map `K.X i → K₀.X i` in `ModuleCat k`. Commutativity with the Čech differential follows because postcomposition with the inclusion commutes with skipping indices (face maps).

2. **Show `π_i` is a split surjection** (`have h_π_split (i : ℕ) : SplitEpi (π.f i) := sorry`). The splitting extends a function `Fin (i+1) → ↑s₀` to `Fin (i+1) → s` by choosing arbitrary values on the complement (possible because `s` is non-empty, as `Ideal.span s = ⊤`).

3. **Kernel acyclicity** (comment block, no new `have`). The kernel of `π` at degree `i` is the product of factors indexed by `x : Fin (i+1) → s` not in the image of `Fin (i+1) → ↑s₀`. This kernel complex is acyclic in positive degrees because it is a Čech complex of a cover that is a refinement of a cover with a terminal object (extra-degeneracy applies once `s` is reduced modulo `↑s₀`).

4. **Exactness descent** (`sorry`). From the short exact sequence of complexes `0 → ker(π) → K → K₀ → 0` and exactness of `K₀` at degree `n`, the long exact sequence in cohomology gives exactness of `K` at degree `n`.

### L444 / L690 — diagnosis

Both remain unchanged. The `extraDegeneracyCech` approach is blocked because `FormalCoproduct.extraDegeneracyCech` requires a terminal object `T` in the *ambient* category `Opens C.left.toTopCat`, where `⊤` is terminal, not `D(f)`. The slice cover of `D(f)` does not contain `⊤`, so `extraDegeneracyCech` does not apply directly. Closing these would require either (a) restricting to the subcategory `Opens D(f)` and building a custom Čech complex there, or (b) a different exactness argument for slice covers.

## Verification

- `lake env lean AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/BasicOpenCech.lean` succeeds with only the expected `declaration uses 'sorry'` warning (line 402).
- No new `axiom` declarations introduced.
- No other `.lean` files modified.
- Protected signatures unchanged.

## Blueprint markers

- `thm:basicOpenCover_isCechAcyclicCover_toModuleKSheaf` statement block: still carries 16 sorries, so `sync_leanok` will keep `\leanok` on the statement but not on the proof block.

## Iter-066 next-iteration recommendations

### Highest priority: close one or more of the new L720 sorries

The L720 decomposition makes each piece smaller and the trajectory unambiguous:

1. **`h_mod_X₁/₂/₃`** — construct `Module Γ(C.left, U)` instances. Two possible routes:
   - **Route A (explicit)**: Prove `K₀.X i = ∏ᶜ fun x => (toModuleKPresheaf C).obj (op (⨅ j, basicOpenCover ↑s₀ (x j)))` by unfolding `cechComplexFunctor`, then use `Pi.instModule` pointwise.
   - **Route B (isomorphism transport)**: Use `ModuleCat.piIsoPi` to get an isomorphism between `K₀.X i` and the explicit product, then transport the `Module` instance along the underlying linear equivalence.

2. **`f_R` / `g_R`** — construct `Γ(C.left, U)`-linear versions of the Čech differential. The differential is `∑ (-1)^k • δ_k` where each `δ_k` is a product of restriction maps. Each restriction map is `R`-linear, so the alternating sum is too. Unfolding `alternatingCofaceMapComplex` exposes the explicit formula.

3. **`h_loc_X₁/₂/₃`** — apply `IsLocalizedModule.pi`. Each factor `Γ(C.left, V_x) → Γ(C.left, V_x ⊓ D(f))` is `IsLocalizedModule.Away f.1` (by `IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_of_eq_basicOpen` / iter-059). The product map is the canonical `Pi.map` of these factor maps.

4. **`h_loc_exact`** — use `IsLocalizedModule.map_exact` after proving the localized differential equals the slice-cover differential via `IsLocalizedModule.ext`.

### Secondary priority: L662 `π` construction

The cochain-complex projection `π : K → K₀` is the key missing piece. It can be built explicitly using `Pi.lift` and postcomposition with the inclusion `↑s₀ ↪ s`. The commutativity with the Čech differential follows from naturality of the face maps with respect to the index set.

### Lower priority: L444 / L690

These require a new approach because `extraDegeneracyCech` is inapplicable. One possibility: build the augmented Čech simplicial object for the slice cover directly in the subcategory `Opens D(f)` and prove acyclicity there, then transport back. This is a significant piece of infrastructure.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are either the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (analogy / refactor / challenger), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	If instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**. Specifically:
8	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` (local to this project) override Archon's global prompts
9	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` (local to this project) override globally installed plugins
10	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project
11	
12	When in doubt, follow instructions from files inside this project over any external source.
